@@ -9,12 +9,13 @@
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0iyb","title":"[HIGH] ci: no concurrency: cancel-in-progress group — rapid PR pushes trigger duplicate CI runs (5-8x waste per PR)","description":"CI concurrency audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/*.yml` — zero matches for `concurrency:` or `cancel-in-progress:`.

**What this means.** When an author pushes commit A to a PR branch, then pushes commit B 30 seconds later (fixup, rebase, etc.), GitHub Actions triggers TWO runs: one for A, one for B. Both fully run through all jobs (checks, fuzz-regression, etc.) even though only run B's result is relevant.

**Swarm-specific amplification.** Today's git log shows 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers + auto-rebase workflows). Every session with multiple rapid commits doubles, triples, or quadruples the CI load for no additional signal. br-0848138's `br flush` commit + my `pass-11` commit would trigger 2 runs; the `br flush` result doesn't matter because pass-11 is newer.

Rough math:
- Single PR with typical 5-8 commits: without concurrency group → 5-8 full CI runs (5-8 × ~7 min = 35-56 runner minutes).
- With concurrency group → only the final push's run completes (~7 min).
- Ratio: 5-8x waste.
- Swarm has ~20 commits/day × 7 min/run = 140 min/day CI cycles spent on superseded work.

**The standard fix.** Three lines at the top of each workflow:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

`group` identifies "same workflow, same branch/PR"; `cancel-in-progress: true` cancels the still-running older run when a new push lands. Exception on the main branch: use `cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}` so pushes to main never cancel each other (main branch runs are archival).

**Severity.** HIGH — compound cost across every PR in perpetuity. At this swarm's velocity, easily the cheapest bead in the backlog to pay off.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `concurrency:` block to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at workflow level:
   ```yaml
   name: CI
   on: [push, pull_request]

   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

   jobs:
     checks:
       ...
   ```

2. If any future workflow is added (br-4clx release workflow, br-36qc security workflow, br-ing6 nightly perf), each needs its own `concurrency:` group — or share the `group:` so they serialize / cancel together.

3. Consider a tighter group key for PR runs:
   ```yaml
   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: true
   ```
   The `pull_request.number || github.ref` idiom groups by PR number on PR events (always unique per PR) and by ref on direct pushes. Belt-and-suspenders version.

Blast radius: ci.yml + any future workflow file, 3-4 lines each. Zero source changes, zero test changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-ffhs (CI job splitting) — split jobs each need their own `concurrency:` group OR share one. Coordinate the two PRs.
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — matrix jobs should ALSO inherit the concurrency cancel so one matrix cell doesn't get canceled while another proceeds (generally fine with the above — whole workflow cancels).
- br-4clx (release workflow) — ships a new workflow, needs its own concurrency block.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:24:46.716318082Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","close_reason":"Already satisfied on main: ci.yml workflow-level concurrency block with PR-aware cancel-in-progress is in place since ffhs CI split","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","concurrency","review","waste"]}

Implement the name override for single-index Series, preserve the existing drop=True Series branch, and add focused tests for named series, unnamed series, and collision handling with index/index-name columns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:59.749325004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.544815036Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.543000433Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-0kx7","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: GroupBy.apply result-shape semantics vs pandas 2.x","description":"Parity-gap audit (/reality-check-for-project).

**Scope.** pandas `DataFrame.groupby(keys).apply(func)` calls a user-provided function per group, returns a Series or DataFrame with the group keys as index. Pandas 2.x changed default `include_groups=True→False`; the result shape depends on what `func` returns.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `DataFrame::groupby(...).apply(closure)` — surface check:

```
grep -n "pub fn apply\|GroupBy.*apply" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs  # find fn signatures
```

Gaps to verify:
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a DataFrame (pandas concatenates, preserving group key index).
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a Series of different length per group (pandas allows, produces sparse output).
- `.apply(func, include_groups=False)` — pandas 2.x default.
- Returning a scalar: pandas collapses to `Series` indexed by group keys.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — `.apply` is widely used for custom group logic; silent divergence from pandas result shape breaks user pipelines.

**Fix shape.**
1. Audit `DataFrameGroupBy::apply_*` signatures in fp-groupby.
2. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_groupby_apply.rs (10 inputs: empty groups, single-row groups, scalar-return, Series-return, DataFrame-return, mixed dtypes, ordered vs unordered groups, duplicate group labels, NaN group labels, `include_groups=False`).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["apply","groupby","parity-gap","review"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0l5r","title":"[HIGH] ci: ubuntu-latest only — macOS/Windows cross-platform claim unverified","description":"Cross-platform CI audit.

**Observation.** .github/workflows/ci.yml line 10: `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`. No `strategy.matrix` defining a per-OS test matrix. No macOS runner. No Windows runner.

**Implications.**
- fp-io reads/writes Excel (.xlsx, .xls), CSV, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC. CSV line-ending semantics (LF vs CRLF) differ across OS. Excel / Parquet zip-archive handling via calamine / parquet-crate depends on OS-level file layer on some paths. None of this is tested on Windows, where the failure modes would appear.
- Path separators: fp-io has pub fn read_csv(path: &Path), pub fn read_parquet(path: &Path), etc. On Windows, a fixture path string produced on Linux with "/" may or may not resolve correctly depending on canonicalization. Not tested.
- Endianness: arrow / parquet byte manipulation is all little-endian; future big-endian fixtures or a PowerPC build would regress silently.
- The README advertises "Cross-platform (Linux/macOS/Windows)" under Installation implicitly by describing standard cargo build. Nothing validates that claim.

**Severity.** HIGH — product claim (cross-platform Rust library) is unverified. A Windows user of frankenpandas today is running untested code on every IO surface.

**Fix shape:**

ci.yml gains:
```
jobs:
  checks:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    ...
```

Caveats and cost:
- Windows runners are ~2x slower than Ubuntu. Initial run will take ~12 min instead of 6.
- rch (remote-build helper) likely won't exist on Windows runners. Windows CI would build locally via `cargo test --workspace`.
- cargo-llvm-cov (installed but unused per sibling bead) works on macOS, limited on Windows; Windows job may need to skip the install.
- Some tests may rely on POSIX behavior: `std::fs::set_permissions` chmod bits, symlinks, etc. Expect 0-5 failures on the first Windows run that need per-OS cfg-gating or fixture adjustments. These become trackable beads.
- Excel path resolution on Windows uses \ separators; calamine's `open_workbook` may handle this but needs verification.

Incremental path if 3-OS matrix is too aggressive at once:
1. Start: ubuntu + macos (rch-compatible POSIX).
2. Next: add windows with only a `cargo check` + `cargo test -p fp-types -p fp-columnar -p fp-index` (no IO) to surface path-handling bugs without full IO breakage.
3. Finally: full `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` on windows.

Related: per-OS arrow/parquet/calamine versions don't diverge today (one workspace.dependencies block) — so any divergence in test behavior is a code bug, not a dep bug.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:59:30.233193403Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.122223754Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.119724019Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","cross-platform","review"]}

Wire 'prod' through the multi-agg dispatch and add a regression that checks prefixed output columns and bucketed products.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:24:37.499390195Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:56:50.843688272Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:56:50.841609789Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0xcm","title":"[MEDIUM] dtype: no SparseDtype — pandas sparse storage not supported","description":"/reality-check-for-project gap (parent br-frankenpandas-jbfn).

**Gap.** pandas exposes `pd.SparseDtype(int64/float64/bool)` for memory-efficient storage of mostly-same-value data (fill_value + sparse-index pair). Common in genomic matrices, recommender systems, one-hot encodings.

FrankenPandas DType enum (fp-types/src/lib.rs):
  Null / Bool / Int64 / Float64 / Utf8 / Categorical / Timedelta64

No SparseDtype variant. grep for `SparseDtype|Sparse` across crates/*/src/ returns zero hits. Every DataFrame materializes dense columns regardless of sparsity.

**Impact.** Users with large sparse datasets (one-hot encoded categorical, DNA methylation matrices, recommender item-user matrices) cannot port pandas code that uses `.to_sparse()`, `df.sparse.density`, `SparseArray` constructors. Memory blows up on O(N×M) allocations where pandas would store O(nnz).

**Fix shape.**
1. Add `DType::Sparse { fill_value: Box<Scalar>, value_dtype: Box<DType> }` variant.
2. Column representation: `enum ColumnStorage { Dense(Vec<Scalar>), Sparse { fill: Scalar, indices: Vec<usize>, values: Vec<Scalar> } }`.
3. Arithmetic ops: `Sparse + Sparse` short-circuits on `fill + fill = fill`; `Sparse + Dense` materializes dense.
4. IO: Parquet already supports sparse via arrow; Feather similar; CSV materializes dense.
5. `Series.sparse` accessor matching `pd.Series.sparse` (density, to_dense, from_dense).
6. DISCREPANCIES.md entry until implementation complete.

**Blast radius.** ~500 LOC in fp-columnar (Column representation + arithmetic), ~200 LOC in fp-io (arrow round-trip), ~300 LOC in fp-frame (sparse accessor), ~30 fixtures in fp-conformance. No breaking changes to existing dense-path code.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — pandas parity claim stands for common operations; sparse is a power-user feature. README's "Full parity target" is the only claim affected, and it's already qualified.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-24T23:33:22.036510171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T00:47:14.611006759Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T00:47:14.608545560Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["dtype","parity-gap","review","sparse"]}

Implement compressed sparse column storage for SparseDType: fill value plus non-fill indices/values, dense materialization, dtype-preserving null handling, and arithmetic/materialization tests.  {"id":"frankenpandas-0yz7","title":"[REALITY-CHECK HIGH] MultiIndex marked Yes-foundation but row MultiIndex absent","description":"README capability table: MultiIndex Yes (foundation) — note the foundation qualifier. Reality: br-r4au tried to ship row MultiIndex emission for grouped rolling/resample by encoding tuples as flat Utf8 strings (a, 2024-01) because real row MultiIndex is not wired through DataFrame; br-ch4o reverted that workaround back to group-key column + bucket-only flat index. Concrete absences: (1) DataFrame::index returns Index<IndexLabel> not MultiIndex<Vec<IndexLabel>>; (2) groupby([k1,k2]).sum output uses string-flattened keys like s:a|i:1 (per FP-P2C-005, FP-P2C-011) not real (k1, k2) tuples; (3) no .loc[(a, 1)] tuple-key indexing; (4) stack/unstack and reset_index for MultiIndex frames are scaffolded but not behaviorally complete. Suggested: split the README MultiIndex row into Column MultiIndex (Yes-foundation) and Row MultiIndex (No), file an epic for genuine row MultiIndex.","notes":"cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22 (commit 14a74c6): split the conflated 'MultiIndex | Yes (foundation)' capability table row into Row MultiIndex (Scaffolded) and Column MultiIndex (Yes-foundation), matching the actual in-tree reality after br-ch4o reverted br-r4au's tuple-emission attempt. MultiIndex section prose (README.md:396) now names the flattening mechanism ('a|1' separator via set_index_multi) and enumerates the specific scaffolded surfaces (tuple .loc, row-MultiIndex groupby output, stack/unstack, reset_index round-trip) with cross-references to the capability table and Limitations. Existing Limitations entry (README.md:1372) already honest; kept. Follow-up epic filed as br-frankenpandas-1zzp covering the full row-axis integration work (6 slices: DataFrame row-axis type, multi-key GroupBy output, tuple indexing, reshape round-trips, IO multi-row index block, live-oracle coverage).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:23:14.932108470Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:38.676116789Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:38.673534621Z","closed_by_session":"cc-pandas-0yz7","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-4r56","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: Timedelta arithmetic + TZ-aware datetime ops","description":"Parity-gap audit: datetime + Timedelta arithmetic, TZ-aware operations.

**Scope.** pandas:
- Timestamp + Timedelta → Timestamp.
- Timestamp - Timestamp → Timedelta.
- Timedelta + Timedelta → Timedelta.
- Timedelta * scalar → Timedelta.
- tz-aware: `pd.Timestamp('2024-01-01', tz='US/Eastern') + pd.Timedelta(days=1)` stays in the same tz.
- DST transitions: adding 1 day across a spring-forward boundary changes wall-clock hour but preserves absolute duration.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-types has `Timedelta` namespace with NANOS_PER_* constants + parse(). No Timestamp type surfaced publicly; datetime values live as Utf8-encoded ISO 8601 strings per README Limitations.

Gaps (from README + existing DISCREPANCIES):
- No native Timestamp dtype → TZ-aware arithmetic not possible.
- Timedelta arithmetic: `Timedelta + Timedelta` not exposed as a pub fn; only internals.
- Scalar::Timedelta64 exists but no `Scalar::Timestamp` / `Scalar::DatetimeTz`.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — datetime handling is pandas's foundational surface; partial coverage limits financial/time-series workflows. chrono-tz is already a workspace dep per Cargo.toml inspection, so IANA tz data is available.

**Fix shape.** Phased:
- Phase 1: expose Timedelta + Timedelta arithmetic as pub fns in fp-types.
- Phase 2: add Timestamp struct (i64 nanos since Unix epoch, optional chrono_tz::Tz).
- Phase 3: live-oracle fixtures for dt arithmetic: Timestamp + Timedelta, Timestamp - Timestamp, tz-aware shift across DST, leap-second handling.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:12:19.958740286Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:14:08.379332198Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datetime","parity-gap","review","timedelta","tz"]}

Implemented Timedelta struct with parsing, formatting, components. Fixed match arms in fp-types, fp-columnar, fp-frame. Agents fixing fp-io and fp-groupby.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-17T23:34:53.669968299Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-17T23:53:03.676385833Z","closed_at":"2026-04-17T23:53:03.674464426Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-8opp","title":"[LOW] citation: no CITATION.cff — scientific users can't mechanically cite frankenpandas in papers","description":"Academic-citation metadata audit.

**Observation.** `ls CITATION.cff` → No such file. No README section on "how to cite this in a paper." No DOI / Zenodo integration.

**Why this matters for a pandas-parity library.** pandas itself is cited in ~50k academic papers per year — it's the canonical data-analysis tool in scientific Python. frankenpandas positions itself as "clean-room Rust reimplementation of the full pandas API surface" targeting the same user base. Scientific users WILL want to cite the library in their methods sections once adoption grows.

**What CITATION.cff is.** YAML-format standard for software citation metadata. GitHub auto-detects the file and renders a "Cite this repository" button + generates BibTeX / APA / etc. on demand.

**Severity.** LOW — no functional impact, no release blocker, no swarm-velocity friction. But: if we ship 0.1.0 to crates.io without a citation format, the first academic user to adopt the library opens an issue asking "how do I cite this?" — easy to preempt.

**Fix shape.** `CITATION.cff` at repo root:
```yaml
cff-version: 1.2.0
message: "If you use frankenpandas in published work, please cite it."
title: "frankenpandas: clean-room Rust reimplementation of the pandas API"
authors:
  - family-names: "Doodle"
    given-names: "Jeffrey E"    # or whichever upstream maintainer names
    orcid: ""                    # optional
repository-code: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
url: "https://frankenpandas.dev"
license: MIT
version: "0.1.0"
date-released: "2026-XX-XX"       # fill in at release
keywords:
  - dataframe
  - pandas
  - rust
  - data-analysis
  - columnar-storage
preferred-citation:
  type: software
  authors: <as above>
  title: "frankenpandas"
  url: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
```

Once GitHub renders the "Cite this repository" button, academic users get BibTeX they can paste into papers. Zero ongoing maintenance beyond version bumps at release.

Optional companion: Zenodo integration. Push a release tag → Zenodo mints a DOI → CITATION.cff updates with the DOI → papers can cite the specific version. Free; takes ~5 min of setup once.

Blast radius: 1 new file (~20 lines). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow) — CITATION.cff's version field updates per release; consider automation.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — "How do I cite this?" section links to CITATION.cff.
- README capability table — "Cite this repository" GitHub button is a free marketing surface.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T22:01:08.609599866Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:51:18.242696Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:51:18.242696Z","close_reason":"CITATION.cff already shipped on main.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["academic","citation","hygiene","review"]}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-8qdg","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: Categorical ordered comparison + sort semantics","description":"Parity-gap audit: Categorical dtype + ordered comparisons.

**Scope.** pandas `pd.Categorical(values, categories=['low','med','high'], ordered=True)` supports:
- `cat1 < cat2` element-wise comparison (valid only on ordered Categoricals).
- `cat.sort_values()` respects category order, not lex.
- `cat.min() / .max()` respects category order.
- Unordered Categorical: comparison raises TypeError.
- `cat.reorder_categories(new_order, ordered=True)` permits comparison after reorder.
- `pd.concat([cat1, cat2])`: if categories differ, promotes to object dtype (pandas 2.x) — DISC candidate.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `CategoricalAccessor` + `CategoricalMetadata` (re-exported via crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:29). Check:

```
grep -n "ordered\|is_ordered\|CategoricalMetadata" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs | head
grep -n "categorical.*cmp\|cat.*compare" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs | head
```

Gaps to verify:
- Does ordered comparison respect category order or fall back to lex / underlying codes?
- Does `.sort_values` on ordered Categorical respect category order?
- Does `.min() / .max()` on ordered Categorical return the earliest/latest category, not lex min/max of values?
- Unordered Categorical `<` behavior: does it return Err or silently produce bogus result?

**Severity.** MEDIUM — Categorical is claimed in README capability table as "metadata layer"; ordered semantics are the point.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_categorical_ordered.rs: 10 inputs covering ordered cmp, unordered cmp rejection, sort_values on ordered, min/max on ordered, reorder_categories then compare, concat with different categories, NaN in categorical, empty categorical, single-category ordered cmp, ordered vs tz-aware (rejection expected).
2. DISCREPANCIES entries for any intentional pandas-2.x divergence.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:12:47.053950440Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:12:47.053950440Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["categorical","ordered","parity-gap","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-ing6","title":"[HIGH] ci: no performance regression gate — 'cargo bench' is a no-op, perf_baselines.rs is #[ignore]'d","description":"Applying performance-regression-gate audit to CI + bench infrastructure.

**Observation.**
- ci.yml line 46 runs `cargo bench`. Workspace has NO `[[bench]]` targets in any Cargo.toml (grep for `\[\[bench\]\]` or `criterion` returns empty). So `cargo bench` is currently a no-op smoke test that always passes.
- crates/fp-conformance/tests/perf_baselines.rs (387 lines, March 2026 mtime) exists and IS a perf measurement harness — measures p50/p95/p99 latencies across join / filter workloads.
- BUT: perf_baselines.rs tests are marked `#[ignore]` by default (see the rustdoc header comment). CI does not pass `--include-ignored`. So perf_baselines never runs in CI either.
- Even when run, perf_baselines only `eprintln!` the results. There are zero `assert!(p95 < Duration::from_millis(X))` calls. It's a human-readable benchmark, not a regression gate.
- No stored baseline file (no `artifacts/perf/baseline.json` or similar). Diffing against prior runs is impossible.

**Consequence.** A 10x slowdown in any hot path lands silently. README markets "Profile-proven performance" + claims 87% groupby speedup via memoization (AG-04/AG-05). Nothing enforces that the speedup persists. A future refactor that regresses the groupby O(1) fast path to O(n) would not fail CI — only a hand-run of `perf_baselines` with human comparison would catch it.

**Severity.** HIGH — core product claim is unguarded against regression. Performance claims that aren't measured in CI become theater.

**Fix shape:**
1. Convert perf_baselines.rs to assertion form: each benchmark gains `assert!(p95 < SOME_BUDGET)`. Budgets live in an `assets/perf_budgets.json` committed to the repo. Exceeding budget = fail CI.
2. Remove `#[ignore]` from perf_baselines.rs so tests run in `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`. Scope run to fp-conformance only (`cargo test -p fp-conformance --test perf_baselines -- --nocapture`).
3. OR (preferred): add a real `[[bench]]` block using `criterion` crate. Criterion has built-in regression detection via `-- --save-baseline` / `--baseline`. CI:
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --save-baseline pr
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --baseline main --threshold 5%
     step: fail on > 5% regression
4. Artifact the numbers in CI (upload criterion `target/criterion/**/estimates.json`) so trending is visible over time.
5. Remove the stub `cargo bench` line from ci.yml (currently misleading — it appears to run a benchmark gate but doesn't).

Blast radius: perf_baselines.rs (+30 assertions), assets/perf_budgets.json (+1 file), ci.yml (replace line 46). If going with criterion: crates/fp-conformance/benches/perf.rs (+~200 lines), Cargo.toml (+criterion dep).

Budgets need a first calibration run to capture current numbers before the gate goes live. Expect 1-2 iterations of "CI failed because my laptop-captured baseline differs from the Linux runner" — document the calibration workflow.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:27:46.851711998Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T22:21:33.159426153Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T22:21:33.157057426Z","close_reason":"Replaced no-op cargo bench with a real budgeted perf regression gate and committed perf budgets","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","performance","regression-gate","review"]}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-iosd","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: Parquet nullable Int64 + JSONL orient=records edge cases","description":"Parity-gap audit: IO format edge cases (Parquet nullable, JSON orient='records' lines).

**Scope.** Two concrete IO edge cases where pandas output shape is subtle and FrankenPandas coverage may diverge:

1. **Parquet nullable Int64 round-trip.** pandas `pd.Int64Dtype()` produces parquet logical INT64 + optional repetition. On read-back, pandas infers `Int64` if the schema has null-markers; legacy `int64` if not. FrankenPandas's fp-io parquet path uses arrow + standard Int64 — does it preserve null-markers on round-trip, or does it lose them (promoting to Float64 with NaN)?

2. **JSON orient='records' with lines=True (JSONL).** pandas `pd.read_json(path, orient='records', lines=True)` reads JSONL where each line is a dict. `pd.to_json(orient='records', lines=True)` writes JSONL. Edge cases:
   - Blank lines in input (pandas: skip; tolerance unspecified).
   - Trailing newline vs no trailing newline.
   - Mixed-schema lines (some records have extra/missing keys).
   - NaN/None handling in records.

FrankenPandas has `read_jsonl_str` / `write_jsonl_string` per frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:76; blank-line tolerance is claimed in the README IO Format Support table ("One object per line, blank-line tolerant, union-key detection"). Worth verifying against pandas.

**FrankenPandas state.**
```
grep -n "pub fn read_parquet\|nullable\|Int64Dtype" crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs | head
grep -n "pub fn read_jsonl\|pub fn write_jsonl" crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs | head
```

**Severity.** MEDIUM — IO is claimed at 7+ format parity; round-trip fidelity is the foundational user-facing claim.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_io_edge_cases.rs: Parquet nullable Int64 round-trip, Parquet all-null column round-trip, JSONL with blank lines, JSONL with trailing newline, JSONL mixed-schema (union keys), JSONL NaN/None, JSONL empty file, JSONL single-record file, JSONL UTF-8 BOM.
2. Any divergence → separate [HIGH] review bead per failure case.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:13:18.976912931Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:13:18.976912931Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["io","jsonl","parity-gap","parquet","review"]}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-w4uu","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: MultiIndex .loc multi-axis slicing semantics","description":"Parity-gap audit: MultiIndex `.loc` multi-axis slicing.

**Scope.** pandas `df.loc[(level_0_key, level_1_key), columns]` indexes a row-MultiIndex DataFrame with a tuple. Supports:
- Exact tuple match: `df.loc[('A', 1)]` → Series.
- Partial tuple: `df.loc['A']` → sub-DataFrame (only outer-level filter).
- Slice in tuple: `df.loc[(slice(None), 1)]` → cross-section on inner level.
- pd.IndexSlice builder: `df.loc[pd.IndexSlice[:, 1], :]`.
- Slicing: `df.loc['A':'B', :]` requires sorted MultiIndex; raises `KeyError` otherwise.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-index exposes `MultiIndex` struct and `DataFrame::xs` / `xs_level`. Check:

```
grep -n "pub fn loc\b\|loc_multi\|xs\|IndexSlice" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs
grep -n "pub fn " crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs | head -20
```

DISC-006 documents scaffolded row-MultiIndex status. This bead is the concrete audit of `.loc` tuple semantics.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — MultiIndex is claimed in the README capability table but .loc edge cases are not proven at parity.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_multiindex_loc.rs covering 10 inputs: exact tuple match, partial tuple (outer), partial tuple (inner via slice), sorted-range slice, unsorted-range slice (expect error), pd.IndexSlice form, missing key KeyError, duplicate MultiIndex labels, NaN level values, empty frame.
2. Any divergence → child [HIGH] bead per failure case.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:52.107787752Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:52.107787752Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["loc","multiindex","parity-gap","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-weh1","title":"[LOW] community: no .github/FUNDING.yml — no Sponsor button / funding channels declared","description":"GitHub Sponsors / funding configuration audit.

**Observation.** `ls .github/FUNDING.yml` → No such file. No declared funding channels (GitHub Sponsors / Open Collective / Liberapay / etc.).

**Context.** For an open-source library targeting a sizable user base (pandas has tens of millions of users; frankenpandas targets their Rust-curious subset), a documented funding mechanism is a cheap way to let engaged users contribute financially. GitHub renders a "Sponsor" button on the repo header when `FUNDING.yml` is present; without it, the button is absent and engaged users have no path.

**Severity.** LOW — no operational impact. No release blocker. No code quality issue. Filing truthfully as LOW; user-visible polish, optional feature.

**Fix shape.** `.github/FUNDING.yml`:
```yaml
# Map the repo's "Sponsor" button to one or more funding platforms.
# Each line enables a platform. Remove entries that don't apply.

github: [<org-or-user>]           # GitHub Sponsors
# open_collective: frankenpandas
# liberapay: <name>
# ko_fi: <name>
# custom: ["https://frankenpandas.dev/sponsor"]
```

Requires a decision: does the maintainer have an active GitHub Sponsors account? If yes, one-line file. If not, skip this bead entirely.

Blast radius: 1 tiny file (< 10 lines), zero code changes.

Prerequisites: Maintainer decides which funding channel(s) to expose. Nothing else.

Related:
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md), br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — same .github/ polish tier.
